patient.instructions: You are LiXX’s cooperative parent, speaking clearly and calmly but worried. Start with: “My child keeps getting stiff and is falling behind other kids, and their face looks puffier lately.” Early in the visit, volunteer: morning joint stiffness and limited bending; shorter than peers with slowed growth; bigger, swollen-looking belly; easy fatigue and not keeping up in play; face looks coarser/puffier than last year. If asked about other issues, mention: frequent ear infections and possible hearing trouble; loud snoring with brief breathing pauses; gets out of breath with stairs/running; weak hands with dropping things and finger tingling; prior hernia bulges near belly button/groin. Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: cloudy vision/bumping in dim light; tight wrists and trouble turning knobs; constipation; slow chewing; slower speech/learning. Withhold bedwetting unless directly asked; if asked, admit embarrassment and say it happens most nights. Express misconceptions: you wondered about juvenile arthritis; thought belly was diet “bloating”; family says it’s “baby fat” and they’ll grow out of it. Raise concerns about insurance denying genetic tests/specialists and costs/time off work. If clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more open.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Mucolipidosis (lysosomal storage disorder; most consistent with mucolipidosis type II/III spectrum)
acceptable_alternatives: Lysosomal storage disorder (unspecified) pending confirmatory testing
required_evidence: Progressive multisystem features consistent with lysosomal storage disease (e.g., coarse facial features, skeletal dysplasia/joint contractures, short stature, developmental delay, organomegaly, recurrent respiratory/ear infections)
No single abnormality on basic CBC/CMP explaining presentation; need targeted metabolic/genetic evaluation

Confirmatory testing plan documented (e.g., lysosomal enzyme studies showing elevated multiple lysosomal hydrolases in plasma with low activity in fibroblasts; GNPTAB/GNPTG genetic testing; radiographic evidence of dysostosis multiplex)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal to mildly low (mild anemia may be present but not diagnostic)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Usually normal (normocytic) unless concurrent iron deficiency/inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal; may be intermittently elevated with intercurrent infections
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel - AST: Usually normal to mildly elevated (nonspecific; may rise with hepatic involvement or muscle turnover)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel - Alkaline: May be elevated, especially in children due to bone turnover/skeletal disease (nonspecific)

non_pharmacological: Refer to genetics/metabolic specialist for definitive diagnosis and counseling (enzyme assays and GNPTAB/GNPTG testing)

Vaccinations up to date; infection prevention strategies; prompt evaluation/treatment for respiratory infections
Discuss prognosis and goals of care; connect family with social work and patient support resources
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Genetics/metabolic referral within 2-4 weeks; sooner (1 week) if rapid progression, feeding/respiratory compromise, or significant pain

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 42-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking accounting manager. Open with: “I’ve been getting more and more unsteady on my feet, like I’m drunk even when I haven’t had anything to drink.” Early in the interview, volunteer: veering to one side when walking, tripping/fear of falling, clumsy hands/missing what you reach for, and slow/slurred speech when tired. If asked, add: brief jittery vision/eyes not steady, occasional coughing/choking with water, trouble with rapid finger taps, messier/larger handwriting, and gradual worsening over 2 years (not episodic). Do not volunteer hidden issues unless specifically prompted: fatigue/mental effort to walk straight, worse in dark/uneven ground, brief spinning with quick head turns, nighttime foot numbness, mild planning/multitasking difficulty. Withhold embarrassing symptoms (urinary accidents; low libido/sexual function) unless asked directly and privately—then answer briefly, with discomfort. Express misconceptions: suspect inner ear problem, stress/anxiety, tried gluten-free without benefit. Mention cost/work concerns only if discussing tests/therapy/accommodations. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and share more detail.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accounting manager. Open with: “I’ve been getting more and more unsteady on my feet, and I’m scared I’m going to fall.” Early in the visit, volunteer that you feel wobbly “like I’m drunk,” trip/bump into doorways, your hands are clumsier (buttons/texting), and your speech can be slurred when tired. Emphasize it’s been slowly worsening over months to years.

If asked about specifics, mention needing railings on stairs (slower down than up), intention tremor/overshooting when reaching, eyes “jumping” when reading/looking side to side, two falls in 6 months, past seizures on medication, and diabetes with worse control.

Do NOT volunteer unless directly asked: choking/coughing on thin liquids, lightheadedness on standing, fatigue-related worsening, numb/tingly feet, bladder urgency.

Resist disclosing accidents/incontinence, avoiding outings from embarrassment, and sexual performance issues; admit only with gentle, direct questioning and reassurance.

Express misconceptions: worry about low blood sugar, inner ear problem, stroke, and that your OTC “nerve support” might help. Voice cost/insurance/job-discrimination fears; downplay PT and resist cane/walker. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and share more.

medications: Insulin glargine 22 units subcutaneously nightly
Insulin lispro 6 units subcutaneously with meals (plus correction scale)
Levetiracetam 750 mg orally twice daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg orally nightly
Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily
Magnesium oxide 400 mg orally daily
confounders: Chronic levetiracetam use with intermittent dizziness and fatigue that can be misattributed to ataxia progression
Suboptimal glycemic control with occasional hypoglycemia causing transient gait instability, tremor, and confusion
Mild hypomagnesemia which can worsen tremor, weakness, and neuromuscular irritability
Peripheral neuropathy risk from long-standing diabetes contributing to balance impairment (sensory ataxia overlap)
History of focal seizures; post-ictal incoordination can mimic episodic ataxia

correct_diagnosis.primary: Spinocerebellar ataxia (hereditary cerebellar ataxia)
acceptable_alternatives: Autosomal dominant spinocerebellar ataxia (SCA, unspecified subtype)
Cerebellar ataxia (chronic progressive), pending genetic confirmation
required_evidence: Progressive gait and limb ataxia with cerebellar signs (e.g., dysmetria, intention tremor, nystagmus, scanning dysarthria)
Chronic/progressive course (months to years) rather than acute onset
Family history consistent with inherited ataxia OR onset pattern suggestive of genetic etiology
No evidence of acute metabolic derangement on basic metabolic panel that would better explain symptoms
Plan for confirmatory evaluation (neurology referral and genetic testing panel for SCA subtypes; consider brain MRI for cerebellar atrophy)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Typically normal or may show comorbid prediabetes/diabetes; not diagnostic for SCA
expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Typically normal; abnormal values suggest comorbid dysglycemia rather than cause of progressive cerebellar syndrome

medications: Acetaminophen 500 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
Meclizine 12.5-25 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for vertigo/dizziness (use cautiously due to sedation and fall risk)
non_pharmacological: Neurology referral for confirmatory workup (genetic testing for SCA; consider brain MRI to assess cerebellar atrophy and exclude structural lesions)
Physical therapy focused on balance, gait training, and strength; home exercise program
Occupational therapy for adaptive strategies, assistive devices, and home safety evaluation
Speech-language pathology evaluation for dysarthria and swallowing assessment if symptoms present; aspiration precautions if dysphagia
Fall-risk mitigation: cane/walker as indicated, remove tripping hazards, night lighting, footwear counseling
Avoid alcohol and sedating medications when possible due to worsening ataxia and fall risk
Screen for and address comorbidities that worsen function (e.g., neuropathy, vision problems); review medication list for ataxia-inducing agents (e.g., anticonvulsants, lithium, benzodiazepines)
Discuss genetic counseling and family planning considerations if hereditary disease suspected/confirmed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology appointment within 4-6 weeks; earlier (1-2 weeks) if rapid progression, frequent falls, new dysphagia, or new focal neurologic deficits

must_include: Spinocerebellar ataxia is a progressive disorder affecting the cerebellum leading to balance and coordination problems
Diagnosis is clinical but confirmation typically involves neurology evaluation, brain imaging, and genetic testing
Acknowledge uncertainty about subtype and need for further testing
No definitive cure for most SCA types; treatment focuses on symptom management and maintaining function
Role of PT/OT/speech therapy and assistive devices to reduce falls and improve daily activities
Medication plan is for symptomatic relief only and may cause sedation; stop/seek help if worsening dizziness, confusion, or increased falls
Discuss prognosis variability and that progression rate differs by subtype and individual
Discuss safety planning for falls and when to seek urgent care (new severe weakness, sudden onset symptoms, repeated falls with injury, choking/aspiration signs)
Offer genetic counseling discussion for patient and family if hereditary form suspected
Avoid alcohol and minimize sedating medications/substances to reduce worsening ataxia and fall risk
Home safety modifications and consistent use of recommended mobility aids
Encourage regular supervised exercise/therapy and sleep optimization

Order/arrange neuroimaging (brain MRI preferred) and/or neurology referral testing pathway; imaging helps exclude structural, inflammatory, demyelinating, or neoplastic causes and supports cerebellar atrophy assessment.
Review available results (if benchmark provides) and integrate into differential; identify abnormalities that change management or urgency.
Provide working diagnosis of suspected spinocerebellar ataxia (or progressive cerebellar ataxia) while acknowledging subtype uncertainty and need for genetic testing/neurology confirmation.

Required safety check before any prescribing for symptomatic therapy (e.g., spasticity, tremor, neuropathic pain, sleep, mood).
Required safety check before prescribing to avoid harmful interactions (especially with sedatives, CNS depressants, antidepressants, anticonvulsants).

If the benchmark includes OTC/substance use (alcohol, cannabis, antihistamines), ensure interaction considerations are explicitly checked/handled prior to prescribing sedating agents.
Required safety check before prescribing (e.g., pregnancy status where relevant, hepatic/renal impairment, respiratory risk, history of falls, cognitive impairment).
Offer symptomatic-only medication plan when indicated; include counseling on sedation/falls and instructions to stop/seek help for worsening dizziness, confusion, or increased falls.
Arrange neurology follow-up and therapy services (PT/OT/speech), review test results, reassess safety/falls, and refine diagnosis with genetic counseling if indicated.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and providing return/ED precautions.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 19-year-old anxious but clear-speaking college student/barista. Start with: “I get tired and weak really fast, especially in my arms and legs, and it gets worse as the day goes on.” Volunteer early: mornings are better; by afternoon legs feel heavy and stairs are hard; arms give out washing hair/holding things up; eyelids sometimes droop when tired; rest helps briefly but weakness returns with activity. If asked about eyes/speech/swallowing/breathing, share: occasional double vision late day; slurred/mushy speech after talking; jaw tires with tough foods; swallowing feels slow; short of breath with exertion; colds/fever worsen everything. Don’t volunteer at first: lifelong “floppy/low-energy,” voice fading, needing breaks while eating, bright light/screens worsen eyes, post-exercise feels like muscles stop. Resist mentioning choking on water/saliva and trouble taking deep breaths lying flat unless directly asked; admit embarrassment about dating/social meals if rapport is strong. Offer misconceptions: worry it’s nerve/muscle/spine/vocal cord problem (foot slapping, calf looks different, hoarseness, stiffness). Emphasize cost fears and belief tests/therapy are optional; fear losing job if missing shifts. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, give more detail, and ask about affordable options.

albuterol HFA 90 mcg/inhalation 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed for wheeze
ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day

recent macrolide exposure associated with symptom worsening (drug-induced neuromuscular transmission impairment)
anxiety-related dyspnea/panic episodes complicating assessment of respiratory muscle weakness
mild asthma symptoms overlapping with exertional dyspnea from neuromuscular weakness
iron deficiency contributing to fatigue independent of neuromuscular junction dysfunction

differentials: distal myopathy with vocal cord weakness

correct_diagnosis.primary: Postsynaptic congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (postsynaptic neuromuscular junction disorder)
Postsynaptic neuromuscular junction transmission defect (congenital myasthenic syndrome)
required_evidence: Chronic, fluctuating fatigable weakness (often ocular/bulbar/limb) beginning in childhood or early life with preserved sensation
Worsening with exertion and improvement with rest; possible episodic respiratory/bulbar exacerbations
Electrophysiology consistent with neuromuscular junction transmission defect (e.g., decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
Negative acetylcholine receptor and MuSK antibodies (supports congenital rather than autoimmune myasthenia)

Exclusion of primary myopathy pattern as dominant finding (e.g., absence of fixed progressive weakness with myopathic EMG predominance) and exclusion of upper motor neuron signs as primary (argues against hereditary spastic paraplegia)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness (feet/ankles > hands) with distal muscle wasting
Distal sensory loss (vibration/proprioception) and reduced/absent ankle reflexes
Foot deformities consistent with CMT (high arches/pes cavus, hammer toes) and/or frequent ankle sprains/foot drop
Family history of similar neuropathy OR onset in childhood/adolescence/early adulthood with long course
No systemic features suggesting acquired neuropathy (e.g., acute onset, marked pain, autonomic failure, systemic inflammatory signs)
Supportive confirmatory testing plan: EMG/NCS showing demyelinating or axonal hereditary pattern and/or genetic testing (e.g., PMP22 duplication, MPZ, GJB1)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected as part of CMT)

medications: Gabapentin 100-300 mg PO at bedtime, titrate every 3-7 days as tolerated to 300 mg PO three times daily for neuropathic pain
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for musculoskeletal pain (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Refer to neurology for confirmation (EMG/NCS and consideration of genetic testing) and subtype classification
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, balance and gait training; home exercise program
Occupational therapy for hand weakness/fine motor adaptations and assistive devices
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) for foot drop and gait stability; supportive footwear/orthotics for pes cavus
Podiatry/orthopedics referral for progressive deformity, recurrent ankle instability, or consideration of corrective surgery
Fall risk assessment and mitigation (assistive device if needed, home safety evaluation)

Avoid/limit neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., vincristine, cisplatin, paclitaxel, nitrofurantoin, metronidazole long-term, linezolid, isoniazid without pyridoxine) and counsel to review new meds with clinicians
Genetic counseling and family screening discussion (inheritance patterns, reproductive options)
Vaccinations and general health maintenance; optimize comorbidities that worsen neuropathy (e.g., diabetes, alcohol use)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks to evaluate pain control, function, gait safety, and therapy/orthotic progress; earlier (1-2 weeks) if medication side effects or rapid functional decline

must_include: CMT is an inherited peripheral nerve disorder causing slowly progressive distal weakness and sensory loss
Typical course is chronic and gradual; severity varies by subtype and person
Diagnosis is supported by exam and confirmed with nerve conduction studies/EMG and often genetic testing
Red flags that should prompt urgent reassessment (rapid progression, new bowel/bladder symptoms, severe asymmetric weakness, significant new pain)
No curative therapy currently; treatment focuses on function, safety, symptom control, and preventing complications
Role of PT/OT and orthotics (AFOs) to improve gait and reduce falls
Neuropathic pain medication plan with titration and expected timeline for benefit
When to consider orthopedic/podiatry evaluation (deformity, instability, ulcers, refractory pain)
Discuss inheritance and implications for family members; offer genetic counseling
Review medication side effects and safety (sedation/dizziness with gabapentin; avoid driving until effects known)
Discuss fall prevention and workplace/school accommodations if needed
Foot care and skin checks (especially with sensory loss) and appropriate footwear
Exercise guidance: low-impact aerobic activity and strengthening within tolerance; avoid overexertion that worsens symptoms
Avoid neurotoxic exposures/medications and excessive alcohol; coordinate with clinicians before starting new drugs

Review lab results (if ordered) to identify alternative causes of neuropathy or contraindications to planned medications (e.g., renal function for gabapentin/pregabalin dosing).

Explain treatment approach: PT/OT, orthotics (AFOs), fall prevention, foot care, and when to refer to orthopedics/podiatry; discuss neuropathic pain medication options and expectations.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication for neuropathic pain or comorbid symptoms.
Non-critical but expected safety step: assess interactions with current meds/supplements (e.g., additive sedation with CNS depressants).

Gather contraindication-relevant details if not already obtained (e.g., renal impairment for gabapentin, pregnancy potential for certain agents, severe depression/suicidality for some antidepressants).
Initiate symptom management when appropriate (e.g., gabapentin with renal-adjusted dosing and titration plan) and document counseling on sedation/dizziness and driving precautions.
Address inheritance/genetic counseling, family implications, workplace/school accommodations, and avoidance of neurotoxic exposures/medications and excess alcohol.

Arrange follow-up to assess response to therapy, adverse effects, fall risk, progression, and completion/results of EMG/NCS/genetic testing or referrals; provide earlier return precautions for red flags.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety-netting, and a clear plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pediatric Acute-onset Neuropsychiatric Syndrome (PANS) with suspected PANDAS subtype (post–Group A Streptococcus trigger)
acceptable_alternatives: PANDAS (Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptococcal infections)
Tourette syndrome or chronic tic disorder
required_evidence: Abrupt, dramatic onset (within 24–72 hours) of OCD and/or severe tics
Age at onset in childhood (typically 3 years to puberty)
Episodic course with acute exacerbations
Temporal association with Group A Streptococcus infection or exposure supported by throat culture/rapid test and/or rising ASO/anti-DNase B titers

Presence of additional neuropsychiatric symptoms (e.g., separation anxiety, emotional lability/irritability, ADHD-like symptoms, sleep disturbance, urinary frequency/enuresis, handwriting deterioration, sensory/motor abnormalities)
Exclusion of alternative neurologic/medical causes (no delirium, focal neuro deficits, meningismus; consider Sydenham chorea if choreiform movements)

expected_lab_results.Rapid strep test (throat swab): Often positive if active pharyngitis; may be negative if infection has resolved
expected_lab_results.Throat culture (Group A Streptococcus): If rapid test negative but suspicion remains, culture may be positive; otherwise negative if no current infection
expected_lab_results.Antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer: May be elevated or rising over 2–4 weeks after GAS infection; single value may be non-diagnostic
expected_lab_results.Anti-DNase B: May be elevated or rising, especially after skin infection; supports recent GAS exposure
expected_lab_results.C-reactive protein (CRP): Usually normal to mildly elevated; marked elevation suggests alternative/invasive infection
expected_lab_results.Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): Usually normal to mildly elevated; marked elevation suggests alternative inflammatory process
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (CBC) - WBC: Normal or mild leukocytosis if active infection

medications: Azithromycin 12 mg/kg PO once daily for 5 days (max 500 mg day 1, then 250 mg daily days 2-5) if confirmed or strongly suspected GAS and non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 40 mg/kg/day)
Sertraline 12.5-25 mg PO once daily for severe OCD/anxiety (start low, titrate every 1-2 weeks as tolerated) OR Fluoxetine 5-10 mg PO once daily (one SSRI only; consider specialist input)
Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly as needed for sleep onset insomnia
non_pharmacological: Treat/confirm GAS infection: obtain throat culture if rapid test negative and clinical suspicion persists; treat confirmed GAS per guidelines
Refer for cognitive behavioral therapy with exposure and response prevention (CBT-ERP) for OCD/tics
Family education on episodic course; symptom tracking; school accommodations (reduced workload during flares, extra time, quiet testing environment)
Screen for and address safety/functional impairment (suicidality, aggression, eating restriction, dehydration); escalate to urgent care/ED if present
Consider pediatric neurology/child psychiatry referral; consider infectious disease/rheumatology if recurrent/atypical course

If severe, refractory symptoms and evidence supports immune-mediated process: discuss specialist-directed options (short NSAID trial, corticosteroids, IVIG/plasmapheresis in select cases) after exclusion of mimics
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks to review culture/serologies, response to antibiotics and symptom trajectory; sooner (48-72 hours) if worsening neuropsychiatric symptoms, dehydration, or safety concerns.

must_include: PANDAS/PANS describes sudden-onset OCD/tics with a possible immune trigger; diagnosis is clinical and requires ruling out other causes
Evidence of recent or current strep infection can support PANDAS but labs may be negative if timing is late; titers indicate exposure not active infection
Symptoms often wax and wane; goal is to treat infections, reduce impairment, and support recovery
If strep is confirmed/suspected, treat with an appropriate non-penicillin antibiotic due to amoxicillin rash history
CBT-ERP is first-line for OCD symptoms; SSRIs may help but start low and monitor for activation/behavioral changes
Expected timelines: infection symptoms improve in days; neuropsychiatric symptoms may take weeks and often need behavioral therapy
Review red flags needing urgent evaluation: suicidal thoughts, severe restriction of eating/drinking, severe aggression, confusion, new neurologic deficits, chorea-like movements
Discuss uncertainty and shared decision-making; avoid unnecessary antibiotics if no evidence of GAS
Plan for school/home supports and caregiver coping strategies during flares
Sleep hygiene and regular routines; limit caffeine; structured day plan
Hand hygiene and reducing exposure to strep; seek evaluation for sore throat/fever in patient and close contacts
Medication adherence and monitoring for side effects (GI upset with azithromycin; mood/activation with SSRIs; NSAID GI/renal cautions)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: abrupt onset OCD/tics, temporal relation to infection, functional impairment, ROS and red flags, prior episodes, meds, allergies (amoxicillin rash), comorbidities, and family goals.

Order testing to evaluate for recent/current GAS and alternative causes when indicated (e.g., throat swab/rapid antigen with culture backup; consider ASO/anti-DNase B; basic labs only if systemic illness/red flags).
Interpret GAS testing and any other labs to guide whether antibiotics are warranted and to support/argue against PANDAS while recognizing limitations of titers and timing.
Make a clinical diagnosis statement (PANS/PANDAS vs alternative) and document rationale and differential; emphasize it is clinical and requires ruling out other causes.
Explain diagnosis (PANDAS/PANS concept, waxing/waning course, role/limits of labs), address uncertainty and shared decision-making, and review red flags requiring urgent evaluation.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm amoxicillin rash history details, severity, and avoid penicillins if concerning for true allergy; select non-penicillin option if treating GAS.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions (e.g., macrolides with QT-prolonging agents; SSRIs with other serotonergic meds).

If GAS confirmed/suspected and benefits outweigh risks, prescribe an appropriate non-penicillin antibiotic; consider symptom-targeted therapy plan (CBT-ERP referral; cautious SSRI start low if indicated).

Explain treatment plan, expected timelines (infection days; neuropsychiatric weeks), side effects/monitoring (GI upset with azithromycin; activation with SSRIs; NSAID cautions), lifestyle and infection-prevention guidance, and school/home supports.
Arrange follow-up to reassess symptoms, adherence, side effects, and need for escalation/referral; provide contingency plan for worsening/red flags.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

Make/communicate working diagnosis of congenital (likely ALAS2-related) X-linked sideroblastic anemia; note need for confirmatory testing (e.g., marrow ring sideroblasts, ALAS2 sequencing) and iron overload assessment.
Critical safety check before prescribing pyridoxine or any adjunct medications.
Non-critical safety check for interactions (e.g., levodopa without carbidopa, isoniazid, other supplements) before prescribing pyridoxine and to review current meds.

Initiate first-line trial of pyridoxine (vitamin B6) for suspected ALAS2-related sideroblastic anemia; avoid empiric iron unless deficiency proven; outline monitoring plan and transfusion thresholds based on symptoms/severity.

Explain diagnosis, inheritance/genetic counseling, treatment expectations/timeline, adverse effects (neuropathy with prolonged high-dose B6), transfusion/iron overload monitoring, lifestyle guidance (avoid alcohol/unreviewed supplements), and urgent return precautions.

Arrange repeat labs to assess response (CBC/retic in ~2–4 weeks; iron parameters as indicated), hematology referral, and genetic counseling/testing; plan ongoing monitoring for iron overload if transfused or iron indices elevated.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and ensuring follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) due to glycosylation pathway defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS) presenting with limb-girdle weakness
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (limb-girdle pattern) due to GMPPB mutation

Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to DPAGT1 mutation
required_evidence: Chronic limb-girdle pattern fatigable weakness with onset in childhood/adolescence or long-standing course
Negative myasthenia autoantibodies (AChR binding antibody negative and MuSK antibody negative)
Creatine kinase normal to mildly elevated (may be mildly elevated in glycosylation-defect CMS, especially GMPPB/GFPT1/DPAGT1)
No biochemical evidence of thyroid dysfunction (TSH and free T4 not consistent with hyper/hypothyroid myopathy)
Liver enzymes not explaining weakness (AST/ALT/ALP not markedly elevated in a pattern suggesting primary hepatic disease as cause of symptoms)
Clinical response to acetylcholinesterase inhibitor and/or 3,4-diaminopyridine supports CMS (if trial performed)
Definitive confirmation by genetic testing of glycosylation-pathway CMS gene (GMPPB, GFPT1, DPAGT1, etc.)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (often < 3–5x ULN); can be intermittently elevated in glycosylation-defect CMS
expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (or not consistent with thyroid myopathy)
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal or mild nonspecific elevation (may track with muscle if CK mildly elevated); no marked hepatocellular pattern
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal or mild nonspecific elevation; no marked hepatocellular pattern

medications: Pyridostigmine 30 mg PO every 6 hours while awake; titrate by 15–30 mg per dose every 3–7 days to effect and tolerability (typical total daily 120–360 mg)

Amifampridine (3,4-diaminopyridine) 5 mg PO three times daily; increase by 5 mg per dose every 3–7 days as tolerated to 10–20 mg PO three to four times daily (max 80 mg/day) if inadequate response to pyridostigmine and no contraindications
non_pharmacological: Refer to neuromuscular specialist for confirmatory testing and phenotype-guided therapy (including genetic testing for CMS glycosylation genes)
Baseline and periodic assessment of respiratory function if symptoms suggest bulbar/respiratory involvement (e.g., FVC, nocturnal hypoventilation screening as indicated)
Physical therapy focused on energy conservation, graded strengthening, and fall prevention; avoid overexertion that provokes prolonged weakness
Medication review to avoid neuromuscular-worsening agents (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, magnesium, some beta-blockers) unless essential and monitored
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptom response and adverse effects in 2–4 weeks after initiating/titrating therapy; earlier (within 1 week) if bulbar/respiratory symptoms, significant side effects, or rapid progression

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 19-year-old anxious but clear-speaking first-year student in a dorm. Start with: “I’ve got the worst headache of my life and I feel really sick.” Immediately volunteer: severe headache that came on over a few hours, high fever/chills, stiff neck (hurts to look down), light sensitivity, repeated vomiting/can’t keep fluids, and feeling confused/out of it.

If asked, add: new purple/red pinpoint rash that doesn’t fade when pressed, diffuse muscle/joint aches, very sleepy/hard to stay awake, sore throat/runny nose for 2 days before, exposure to someone with “flu,” and cold hands/feet.

Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked: rash spreading/bruise-like spots, peeing much less, dizziness on standing, racing heart, blotchy/pale skin.

Be resistant about vaccines and party/drink/vape sharing; say you’re not sure and don’t want to discuss.

Mention you get hemiplegic migraines and had brief arm weakness/tingling; say migraine meds didn’t help and you thought rash was allergy/bug bites.

Express cost fears: ask for outpatient/cheapest antibiotic, suggest stopping early once better, and push for minimal time off. If clinician is empathetic, briefly calm down and answer more directly.

CSF Gram stain.value: Many PMNs; intracellular Gram-negative diplococci seen

medications: Ceftazidime 2 g IV q8h
Topiramate 25 mg PO BID
Acetaminophen 1 g PO/IV q6h PRN fever
0.9% Sodium chloride IV bolus 30 mL/kg once then maintenance per hemodynamics
confounders: Hemiplegic migraine can cause transient unilateral weakness and aphasia, mimicking stroke/meningoencephalitis
Recent NSAID use for headache may partially reduce fever and mask symptom severity
Early meningococcemia can present with nonspecific viral-like prodrome before rash/meningismus becomes evident

correct_diagnosis.primary: Invasive meningococcal disease (meningococcemia with suspected meningitis)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute bacterial meningitis (etiology not yet confirmed)
required_evidence: Acute systemic illness with sepsis features (fever, tachycardia, hypotension or poor perfusion)
Meningeal irritation and/or altered mental status (headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, confusion)
Non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash consistent with meningococcemia (if present)
Marked inflammatory response (elevated WBC with neutrophilia, elevated CRP and/or procalcitonin)
Blood cultures obtained prior to antibiotics when feasible without delaying therapy

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often elevated (leukocytosis) or occasionally low in severe sepsis; overall consistent with acute bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal initially; may be mildly decreased if hemolysis/bleeding or dilution
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: May be decreased (thrombocytopenia) in meningococcemia/DIC; can be normal early
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Sodium: May be low (hyponatremia) due to SIADH in meningitis or stress response
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Potassium: Variable; may be normal or abnormal with dehydration, renal dysfunction, or acidosis

medications: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h
Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h (dose per renal function and trough/AUC targeting)
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h x 4 days (give before or with first antibiotic dose if pneumococcal meningitis is a concern; discontinue if meningococcus confirmed)
non_pharmacological: Do not delay antibiotics for imaging or lumbar puncture if clinically unstable; obtain blood cultures immediately if possible
Droplet isolation precautions until at least 24 hours after effective antibiotic therapy
Aggressive sepsis management: IV crystalloids, vasopressors if needed, oxygen/airway support as indicated
Frequent neurologic and hemodynamic monitoring; consider ICU level care
Arrange chemoprophylaxis for close contacts (e.g., rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone per local guidance) via public health protocols
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate reassessment within 1 hour after antibiotics/fluids; ongoing monitoring continuously/ICU; review cultures and clinical response within 24-48 hours to narrow therapy

must_include: This is a medical emergency: suspected meningococcal infection can progress rapidly
Explain suspected meningitis/sepsis and why immediate IV antibiotics are needed before all tests are back
Explain infection control: droplet precautions and public health notification/partner services
Rationale for empiric therapy (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) and plan to narrow once cultures identify the organism
Expected duration: typically 5-7 days for confirmed meningococcal meningitis (adjust to clinical course and guidance)
Supportive care for sepsis (fluids, possible ICU monitoring) and what triggers escalation
Penicillin allergy history: urticarial rash as a child; discuss low cross-reactivity with third-generation cephalosporins and monitoring for reaction
Potential adverse effects (rash/anaphylaxis, diarrhea, kidney effects with vancomycin) and monitoring plan
Discuss need for contact prophylaxis and what household/close contacts should do
Avoid close contact until at least 24 hours after effective antibiotics; reinforce hygiene/respiratory etiquette
Vaccination counseling after recovery as appropriate (MenACWY and MenB per age/risk factors and local schedule)
Return precautions: worsening headache, confusion, rash progression, breathing issues, decreased urine output, fainting

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX’s anxious caregiver. Speak vaguely and jumpy. Start with: “He’s burning hot since yesterday and now he’s really sleepy… not making sense.” Volunteer: high fever, very hot to touch, hard to wake, confused speech, bad headache with light hurting eyes, vomited twice. If asked about other symptoms, then mention: neck stiffness and pain looking down, a 1‑minute shaking episode with jerking then exhaustion, stumbling/trouble walking, buzzing/ringing in ears, getting worse over hours. Do not mention unless directly asked: peeing very little/dark urine, shallow breathing/pauses during sleep, heart racing then slowing, clammy sweating, many mosquito bites near rice fields/pig farm. Withhold at first (only admit if asked nonjudgmentally): you delayed coming because of vaccine/travel worries; you gave an herbal remedy and some leftover antibiotics. Misconceptions to express: “Maybe it’s just seizures or his development getting worse—he’s been clumsier, quieter, fidgety, and has staring spells.” If clinician shows empathy, soften, share more, but keep worried about costs; ask to leave once fever drops and request the cheapest care.

confounders: Baseline hypoventilation with chronic hypercapnia from ROHHAD can mask early respiratory decline from encephalitis/brainstem involvement
Autonomic dysregulation (episodic hypertension/tachycardia) can mimic sepsis physiology
Mild hyponatremia may be multifactorial (SIADH from CNS infection vs desmopressin effect), complicating interpretation
Obesity increases risk of obstructive sleep apnea and sedation sensitivity, complicating neurologic assessment and airway management

correct_diagnosis.primary: Japanese encephalitis
required_evidence: Acute febrile illness with encephalopathy (altered mental status) and/or seizures
Neurologic findings consistent with encephalitis (e.g., focal deficits, movement disorder, meningismus may be absent)
Epidemiologic risk (residence/travel in endemic area, mosquito exposure, seasonality, rural/agricultural proximity)
Exclusion of alternative causes of encephalopathy (metabolic derangements on CMP not sufficient to explain symptoms)
Confirmatory testing planned/obtained: CSF Japanese encephalitis virus (JEV) IgM (preferred) and/or serum JEV IgM with confirmatory neutralization as available

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal to mildly elevated (e.g., 8-15 x10^9/L); may be normal early
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal for age/sex (no specific pattern)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Typically normal (thrombocytopenia not a defining feature)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be low-normal to mildly low (possible SIADH in CNS infections)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): May be mildly low if lactic acidosis from seizures; otherwise normal

medications: Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV every 8 hours (start empirically until HSV encephalitis excluded; adjust for renal function)
Levetiracetam 20 mg/kg IV loading dose once, then 10 mg/kg IV/PO every 12 hours (if seizures or high seizure risk per presentation)
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/PR every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max per weight-based limits)
Isotonic IV fluids (0.9% saline) titrated to euvolemia; avoid hypotonic fluids if hyponatremia/SIADH suspected
non_pharmacological: Admit to hospital; ICU level care if airway compromise, refractory seizures, or signs of increased intracranial pressure
Airway/breathing/circulation stabilization; continuous cardiorespiratory and neurologic monitoring
Seizure precautions; treat acute seizures per protocol (e.g., benzodiazepine rescue if actively seizing)
Evaluate and manage increased intracranial pressure if suspected (head elevation, avoid hypercapnia; neuroimaging and neurology/critical care consultation)
Infection workup and isolation per local policy; obtain CSF studies including JEV IgM and other encephalitis testing as available
Public health notification if suspected/confirmed per jurisdiction; mosquito avoidance counseling for contacts/community
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours after stabilization/initial therapy; daily inpatient reassessment with neurology and infectious diseases; outpatient follow-up 2-4 weeks post-discharge for neurocognitive/rehabilitation needs

must_include: This is inflammation of the brain (encephalitis) likely due to Japanese encephalitis virus given symptoms and exposure risk
Diagnosis is supported by clinical picture and confirmed with specific antibody testing (CSF/serum JEV IgM); other causes like HSV must be ruled out
Atypical Rett syndrome is a chronic neurodevelopmental condition and does not explain an acute febrile encephalopathy
No proven antiviral therapy for JEV; treatment is supportive and focused on airway, seizures, brain swelling, and complications
Empiric acyclovir is started because HSV encephalitis is treatable and dangerous; it can be stopped if HSV is excluded
Possible need for ICU monitoring and rehabilitation; outcomes vary and neurologic sequelae can occur
Review allergy history (amoxicillin-clavulanate caused urticarial rash) and explain it does not prevent use of current planned medicines unless new reactions occur
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent escalation (worsening consciousness, persistent seizures, breathing difficulty)
Set expectations about testing timelines and uncertainty while awaiting results
Mosquito bite prevention: repellents, bed nets, long sleeves, avoiding peak mosquito hours
Vaccination counseling for future risk/travel to endemic areas (if applicable) and community prevention measures
Post-illness support: neurocognitive assessment, physical/occupational/speech therapy if deficits persist

Interpret results to support JEV and exclude/assess alternative diagnoses (especially HSV) and to guide ongoing management and prognosis.

Provide working diagnosis of suspected Japanese encephalitis (pending confirmation) while maintaining differential and explicitly addressing why chronic conditions (e.g., atypical Rett syndrome) do not explain acute febrile encephalopathy.
Perform required critical safety check before prescribing; confirm allergy details (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash), severity, timing, and any anaphylaxis history.
Optional safety check prior to prescribing to identify clinically relevant interactions with planned empiric antivirals/antiepileptics/sedatives and existing meds.

Initiate appropriate management: empiric IV acyclovir for possible HSV encephalitis plus supportive/ICU-level measures (airway protection, seizure control, intracranial pressure management, fluids/electrolytes) while awaiting confirmatory tests.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis rationale and testing, explain treatment/supportive care and why acyclovir is started, address allergy concern and warning signs, and provide mosquito prevention/vaccine counseling and rehabilitation expectations.
Arrange follow-up for results review, neurologic monitoring, rehabilitation planning, and post-encephalitis neurocognitive assessment; ensure clear escalation plan if deterioration occurs.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing return precautions, and documenting plan.

minimal_paths.description: Standard encephalitis pathway: recognize suspected JEV based on exposure and encephalopathy, send CSF/serum JEV IgM and HSV PCR, start empiric acyclovir, provide supportive/ICU-level care guidance, and deliver required counseling and prevention education.
must_collect: altered mental status level and progression
travel or residence in endemic area and mosquito exposure
allergy history details (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticaria; anaphylaxis history)
renal disease history or baseline kidney function risk
red flags (respiratory compromise, rapidly worsening consciousness)
steps: ASK: focused encephalitis + exposure + meds/allergies + red flags
ORDER_LAB: CSF basic studies + CSF JEV IgM + serum JEV IgM + CSF HSV PCR (± VZV) + CBC/CMP + cultures; consider MRI/EEG
GET_RESULTS: interpret CSF profile and viral studies; reassess differential
DIAGNOSE: suspected Japanese encephalitis pending confirmation; rule out HSV; clarify Rett is not acute cause
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm beta-lactam rash history and relevance to planned meds

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pituitary-region epidermoid or dermoid cyst (sellar/suprasellar inclusion cyst) with possible hypopituitarism
required_evidence: Sellar/suprasellar cystic lesion consistent with dermoid/epidermoid on MRI (e.g., diffusion restriction for epidermoid; T1 hyperintensity/fat signal for dermoid) with pituitary stalk/optic chiasm proximity
Symptoms/signs of mass effect and/or pituitary dysfunction (e.g., headache, visual field deficit, fatigue, hypotension, hyponatremia, amenorrhea/low libido)
Pituitary axis evaluation demonstrating possible central adrenal insufficiency and/or central hypothyroidism (low/inappropriately normal ACTH with low AM cortisol; low/normal TSH with low free T4)

expected_lab_results.Sodium: May be low (hyponatremia) if secondary adrenal insufficiency and/or hypothyroidism present; can be normal if no endocrine deficit
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Low if hyponatremic (hypotonic hyponatremia); otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Inappropriately high (>100 mOsm/kg) in adrenal insufficiency-related hyponatremia (impaired free water excretion); otherwise variable/normal
expected_lab_results.Urine sodium: Often >30 mmol/L in euvolemic hypotonic hyponatremia from adrenal insufficiency; otherwise variable
expected_lab_results.Morning serum cortisol (8 AM): Low (<3–5 µg/dL strongly suggests adrenal insufficiency) or indeterminate (5–15 µg/dL) requiring confirmatory testing; can be normal if axis intact
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal with low cortisol (central adrenal insufficiency); can be normal if axis intact
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal in central hypothyroidism; can be normal
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Low in central hypothyroidism; can be normal

medications: Hydrocortisone 15 mg PO qAM and 5 mg PO mid-afternoon (start if central adrenal insufficiency suspected/confirmed; give before thyroid hormone)

Formal ophthalmologic evaluation with visual acuity and visual field testing (e.g., automated perimetry) if lesion is near optic chiasm or symptoms present
Patient education on adrenal insufficiency: stress-dose steroids during illness/surgery, medical alert identification, emergency injectable steroid plan if indicated
Hyponatremia management guided by severity/symptoms: fluid restriction if euvolemic and mild; treat underlying adrenal insufficiency; avoid rapid sodium correction
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Endocrinology follow-up within 1–2 weeks (sooner if hyponatremia or low cortisol); neurosurgery evaluation urgently (days–1 week) if visual symptoms or significant mass effect; repeat pituitary labs and sodium within 3–7 days after starting steroids/thyroxine or sooner if symptomatic

must_include: The lesion is a benign inclusion cyst (dermoid/epidermoid) located in/near the pituitary region
Symptoms can come from pressure on nearby structures (especially vision pathways) and from reduced pituitary hormone function
These cysts are not the same as a hormone-secreting pituitary tumor, but can still cause hormone deficiencies
Definitive management often involves neurosurgical evaluation and possible surgical removal/decompression, especially with visual symptoms or progressive growth
If adrenal insufficiency is present or strongly suspected, steroids are started promptly and before thyroid hormone to prevent adrenal crisis
Thyroid hormone replacement is based on free T4 (TSH may be misleading in central hypothyroidism)
Plan for monitoring sodium and symptoms, and when to seek urgent care (worsening headache, vomiting, confusion, fainting, visual changes)
Discuss risks of untreated hormone deficiencies (adrenal crisis, worsening hyponatremia) and risks/benefits of surgery
Clarify expected course: symptoms may improve with hormone replacement and/or surgery; long-term endocrine follow-up may be needed
Confirm penicillin allergy details and reassure it does not usually affect steroid/thyroid therapy
Sick-day rules for adrenal insufficiency: increase steroid dose during fever/vomiting/major stress and seek care if unable to keep medications down
Avoid excessive free-water intake if hyponatremic and follow fluid guidance provided
Importance of adherence and timing (levothyroxine on empty stomach; separate from iron/calcium by at least 4 hours if applicable)

Interpret results to determine presence/likelihood of adrenal insufficiency, central hypothyroidism, other pituitary deficits, and electrolyte abnormalities requiring urgent management.

Check for interactions affecting levothyroxine (iron, calcium, bile acid sequestrants, PPIs, sucralfate) and steroids (CYP3A4 inducers/inhibitors) and adjust counseling/timing.

If adrenal insufficiency is present or strongly suspected, prescribe glucocorticoids promptly and before starting thyroid hormone; if central hypothyroidism confirmed and adrenal status covered, prescribe levothyroxine titrated to free T4; include management plan for hyponatremia if present.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (benign inclusion cyst; symptoms from pressure and hormone deficits), explain treatment (neurosurgical evaluation; steroid-before-thyroid; free T4-based monitoring; urgent symptoms), address concerns (risks of untreated deficiencies/surgery; course; allergy reassurance), and lifestyle education (sick-day rules, fluid guidance, levothyroxine timing).
Arrange timely neurosurgery and endocrinology follow-up; plan repeat labs (electrolytes, free T4, cortisol/ACTH as appropriate) and visual field/ophthalmology assessment if any visual complaints.
Close encounter after ensuring safety-netting, prescriptions, and follow-up are in place.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pudendal neuralgia (neuropathic pelvic/perineal pain due to pudendal nerve entrapment/irritation)
acceptable_alternatives: Chronic pelvic pain syndrome with predominant neuropathic features (if pudendal distribution and Nantes criteria partially met)
required_evidence: Pain in pudendal nerve territory (perineum/genitals/rectal area), often burning/shooting/tingling
Pain worsens with sitting and improves with standing/lying
No objective sensory loss on exam (or minimal, non-dermatomal findings)
Pain does not wake patient from sleep (classically)
Reproduction of pain with palpation near ischial spine/Alcock canal or positive Tinel-like sign along pudendal course
Exclusion of alternative causes (infection, mass, spinal radiculopathy, gynecologic/urologic/colorectal pathology)
Supportive: pain relief after diagnostic pudendal nerve block

expected_lab_results.CMP_eGFR: Normal or mildly reduced but stable (no acute kidney injury)

medications: Gabapentin 300 mg PO at bedtime, increase by 300 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated toward 300 mg PO three times daily (max per tolerance/renal function)
Duloxetine 30 mg PO daily for 7 days, then 60 mg PO daily (neuropathic pain; avoid if significant hepatic disease)
Lidocaine 5% topical ointment apply thin layer to painful perineal area up to 3 times daily as needed
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower max if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Pelvic floor physical therapy focused on relaxation/down-training (avoid aggressive strengthening if it increases pain)
Activity modification: avoid prolonged sitting; use cut-out cushion; frequent standing breaks
Avoid triggers (cycling/rowing/squats that compress pudendal region) until improved; gradual return as tolerated
Consider image-guided diagnostic/therapeutic pudendal nerve block with local anesthetic ± corticosteroid if symptoms persist despite conservative therapy
If refractory: referral to pain medicine and/or pelvic neurology; consider neuromodulation or surgical decompression in carefully selected cases meeting criteria
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for medication tolerance/response; earlier (within 3-7 days) if adverse effects or worsening pain; consider escalation/interventional referral by 6-8 weeks if inadequate improvement

must_include: Definition: irritation/entrapment of the pudendal nerve causing neuropathic pelvic/perineal pain
Typical pattern: worse with sitting, better with standing/lying; may include genital/rectal/perineal burning or electric pain
Diagnosis is clinical and often supported by response to a pudendal nerve block; labs are usually normal
Important differentials to rule out (infection, gynecologic/urologic/colorectal causes, lumbar radiculopathy)
Stepwise approach: conservative measures + neuropathic pain meds first; procedures if refractory
How to take/titrate gabapentin and common side effects (sleepiness, dizziness); caution with driving/alcohol/sedatives
Duloxetine purpose and common side effects (nausea, dry mouth, sleep changes); do not stop abruptly; discuss serotonin syndrome warning if combined with other serotonergic agents
Topical lidocaine use and local skin irritation precautions
Expected timeline: partial improvement over weeks; goal is functional improvement and pain reduction
Validate pain experience and chronicity; set realistic expectations
Red flags requiring urgent evaluation (new numbness/weakness, saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder dysfunction, fever, unexplained weight loss, rapidly worsening pain)
Discuss medication risks in context of allergies: avoid codeine-containing products; penicillin allergy noted for future antibiotics
Shared decision-making regarding nerve block/referrals if conservative therapy fails
Sitting modifications (cut-out cushion, posture, breaks) and avoidance of compressive activities (cycling) during flare
Pelvic floor relaxation strategies and adherence to PT
Sleep, stress management, and gradual activity pacing to reduce flares
When and how to follow up; keeping a pain/trigger diary

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history of pelvic/perineal neuropathic pain pattern and rule out red flags/alternate etiologies (infection, gynecologic/urologic/colorectal, lumbar radiculopathy).

Clarify pain characteristics (burning/electric), distribution (genital/rectal/perineal), triggers (worse sitting, better standing/lying), functional impact, prior treatments, and relevant PMH/psychosocial factors.

If indicated by symptoms, order targeted tests to exclude infection/inflammation (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture, pregnancy test when relevant); acknowledge that routine labs are often normal in pudendal neuralgia.
Review ordered test results before finalizing plan; confirm no alternative diagnosis requiring different management.
Make a clinical diagnosis of pudendal neuralgia when presentation fits, and note that response to pudendal nerve block can support diagnosis.
Explain diagnosis, key differentials to rule out, and set expectations for a stepwise management plan.
Critical safety check before prescribing; confirm allergies (including opioid/codeine products and penicillin) and reaction details.
Critical safety check before prescribing; screen for serotonergic agents (SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs, triptans, linezolid), CNS depressants, alcohol use, and other interacting meds.

Collect contraindication-relevant history before prescribing (renal function for gabapentin dosing, hepatic disease for duloxetine, uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma, severe hypertension, bipolar/mania history, seizure history, pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations).
Initiate stepwise conservative/neuropathic pain regimen (e.g., gabapentin titration, consider duloxetine, topical lidocaine) with clear instructions, side effects, and safety counseling.

Provide lifestyle guidance (sitting modifications, pelvic floor PT/relaxation, pacing, sleep/stress strategies), red-flag return precautions, and shared decision-making about nerve block/referrals if refractory.
Arrange follow-up to assess response/tolerability (typically 2–6 weeks), reinforce diary tracking, and plan escalation if inadequate relief.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Spinocerebellar ataxia (hereditary cerebellar ataxia)
acceptable_alternatives: Autosomal dominant spinocerebellar ataxia (SCA, subtype unspecified)
required_evidence: Progressive gait and limb ataxia with cerebellar signs (e.g., dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia, nystagmus, scanning dysarthria)
Chronic, slowly progressive course (months to years) rather than acute onset
No metabolic/toxic explanation on basic labs (CBC/CMP/glucose without an alternative cause)
Family history of similar symptoms OR genetic testing consistent with SCA (if available)
Neuroimaging consistent with cerebellar atrophy (if available)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected as primary cause of ataxia)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Typically normal (no macrocytosis expected; macrocytosis would prompt evaluation for B12/folate deficiency as an alternative cause)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal (used for medication safety if prescribing renally cleared drugs)
expected_lab_results.CMP - AST: Typically normal (baseline for hepatically metabolized meds)
expected_lab_results.CMP - ALT: Typically normal (baseline for hepatically metabolized meds)
expected_lab_results.Fasting glucose: Typically normal (screening; hypoglycemia/hyperglycemia not expected to explain progressive cerebellar ataxia)

medications: Baclofen 5 mg PO three times daily (if spasticity present; titrate as tolerated)
Sertraline 25 mg PO daily (if comorbid depression/anxiety; titrate to effect)
non_pharmacological: Refer to neurology (movement disorders/ataxia specialist) for diagnostic confirmation and consideration of genetic testing/counseling
Physical therapy focused on gait/balance training and fall prevention
Occupational therapy for adaptive equipment and activities of daily living support
Speech-language therapy for dysarthria and swallowing evaluation if dysphagia suspected
Assistive devices as needed (cane/walker), home safety evaluation, and fall-risk mitigation
Avoid alcohol and sedating medications that worsen ataxia; review all OTC/supplements for CNS effects
Genetic counseling for patient and family (inheritance patterns, testing implications)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks for symptom control, medication tolerability, falls, and therapy progress; earlier (1-2 weeks) if medication started/titrated or if rapid functional decline occurs.

must_include: Ataxia is due to cerebellar dysfunction causing balance/coordination problems
Spinocerebellar ataxias are often genetic and typically slowly progressive
Diagnosis is clinical and supported by neurology evaluation; genetic testing and/or MRI may be recommended
Discuss uncertainty and the need to rule out treatable mimics (e.g., vitamin deficiencies, thyroid disease, medication/toxin effects) even if basic labs are normal
No definitive cure for most SCAs; treatment focuses on symptom management and function
Role of PT/OT/speech therapy in maintaining mobility, safety, and communication/swallowing
Medication goals (target symptoms such as spasticity, mood symptoms) and common side effects (e.g., baclofen sedation/dizziness; sertraline GI upset/activation)
Importance of fall prevention and assistive devices
Review red flags requiring urgent care (new severe headache, acute focal weakness, sudden worsening, recurrent falls with injury, aspiration/choking, syncope)
Discuss driving/work safety and individualized assessment if gait/coordination impaired
Offer genetic counseling discussion including family planning considerations and implications for relatives

nl_assertions: Agent elicits onset and progression pattern of ataxia and differentiates chronic progressive vs acute/subacute causes.
Agent asks about falls, injuries, and gait safety; assesses dysphagia/aspiration risk and choking episodes.
Agent obtains a comprehensive medication/OTC/supplement and alcohol/toxin exposure history to identify iatrogenic/toxic contributors.
Agent asks about family history suggestive of inherited ataxia and offers genetic counseling discussion.
Agent explicitly states that ataxia is due to cerebellar dysfunction causing balance/coordination problems.
Agent states that spinocerebellar ataxias are often genetic and typically slowly progressive.
Agent explains that diagnosis is clinical and supported by neurology evaluation; genetic testing and/or MRI may be recommended.
Agent discusses uncertainty and the need to rule out treatable mimics (e.g., vitamin deficiencies, thyroid disease, medication/toxin effects) even if basic labs are normal.
Agent explains there is no definitive cure for most SCAs and treatment focuses on symptom management and function.
Agent recommends PT/OT/speech therapy and ties each to functional goals (mobility, safety, communication/swallowing).

If discussing medications, agent frames goals as symptom-targeted (e.g., spasticity, mood symptoms) and mentions common side effects (e.g., baclofen sedation/dizziness; sertraline GI upset/activation).
Agent emphasizes fall prevention strategies and appropriate use of assistive devices.
Agent reviews red flags requiring urgent care: new severe headache, acute focal weakness, sudden worsening, recurrent falls with injury, aspiration/choking, syncope.
Agent discusses driving/work safety and recommends individualized assessment when coordination/gait is impaired.

Agent provides lifestyle guidance: avoid alcohol/recreational drugs; maintain regular exercise within safety limits with supervised balance training; home modifications (remove loose rugs, improve lighting, grab bars); avoid sedating meds (benzodiazepines, antihistamines) unless clearly indicated.
Safety gating: if the agent prescribes, it must perform allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check before prescribing; failure is a critical error.
Agent avoids overpromising; does not claim curative therapy; sets expectations for gradual progression and supportive care.
Agent uses clear, empathetic, jargon-minimized language and checks understanding/teach-back at least once.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize likely chronic cerebellar ataxia consistent with suspected SCA; focus on education, rule-out labs, referrals, and safety without prescribing.
Same as above, plus prescribe a symptom-targeted medication (e.g., baclofen for spasticity or sertraline for mood) after required safety checks.

Agent sequences education earlier (after initial history) and revisits after results, as long as all milestone content is included.
steps: EDUCATE (explain_treatment + lifestyle + red flags + genetic counseling/driving/work)
